attributes.stem:
A 46-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a one-year history of slowly enlarging lesions on both shins. She reports no pain or itching from the lesions but is concerned about their appearance. She is a surfer, and has had two basal cell carcinomas excised from her nose two years prior. She has hypothyroidism and takes thyroxine tablets.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are well-demarcated, waxy, yellow-brown atrophic plaques with prominent telangiectasia on both anterior shins (image). The centres of the plaques appear translucent.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of necrobiosis lipoidica: well-demarcated, waxy, yellow-brown atrophic plaques with telangiectasia on the anterior shins. The non-infectious condition has a strong association with diabetes mellitus, particularly type 1 diabetes, though it can occur in non-diabetic patients, and affects females more so than males. The glycaemic control appears to not correlate to the development of necrobiosis lipoidica. The lesions typically begin as firm, red-brown papules that slowly expand and develop a yellow-brown, atrophic centre with prominent blood vessels, on the lower leg, ankles and feet though can also appear anywhere else on the body.

Pretibial myxoedema is associated with Graves' disease and typically presents as firm, waxy, flesh-coloured or pink nodules and plaques on the shins. While this patient has hypothyroidism, pretibial myxoedema lacks the characteristic atrophy and yellow-brown colour seen in necrobiosis lipoidica.

Morphea (localised scleroderma) presents as circumscribed, indurated plaques that progress from an inflammatory purple border to a sclerotic white centre. While it can cause atrophy, it lacks the characteristic yellow-brown colour and telangiectasia of necrobiosis lipoidica.

Erythema nodosum presents as tender, erythematous nodules typically on the anterior shins. Unlike necrobiosis lipoidica, these lesions are acute, painful, and resolve within weeks to months without residual scarring or atrophy.

Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) typically presents as a firm, tender, erythematous nodule or plaque that may be ulcerated or crusted. While this patient has a history of skin cancer, SCCs are usually solitary lesions and do not present with the bilateral, symmetrical, atrophic appearance characteristic of necrobiosis lipoidica.

The diagnosis of necrobiosis lipoidica is usually clinical, based on the characteristic appearance and distribution of the lesions. A skin biopsy, if performed, shows a layered granulomatous inflammation in the dermis with collagen degeneration.

Image from: Pathak, R., Karmacharya, P., Raj Aryal, M., & Smith-Coleman, K. E. Necrobiosis lipoidica. Journal of Community Hospital Internal Medicine Perspectives. 2013. Available at: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.3402/jchimp.v3i3-4.22627
learningPoints: Necrobiosis lipoidica presents with characteristic yellow-brown atrophic plaques with telangiectasia on the anterior shins.
The condition is associated with diabetes mellitus, particularly type 1 diabetes, though not correlating to the glycaemic control.
references: Pathak, R., Karmacharya, P., Raj Aryal, M., & Smith-Coleman, K. E. Necrobiosis lipoidica. Journal of Community Hospital Internal Medicine Perspectives.  2013.  Available at: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.3402/jchimp.v3i3-4.22627

Zhang D, McMullin G. Necrobiosis lipoidica: a review of management and the role of compression.  Wound Australia Journal. 2021:30 (1).  Available at: https://journals.cambridgemedia.com.au/wpr/volume-30-number-1/necrobiosis-lipoidica-review-management-and-role-compression
